title: disk_corruption
description: A production server disk experiences file system corruption, causing data access failures.

timeline.title: I/O Errors Detected
timeline.description: Application logs begin showing I/O errors and file access failures on the primary data volume.

timeline.title: Database Queries Failing
timeline.description: The database reports corrupted table entries and starts rejecting read/write operations.

timeline.title: Alert Escalated
timeline.description: Monitoring escalates the alert to the on-call DBA and infrastructure team.

timeline.title: Disk Health Check
timeline.description: SMART diagnostics reveal reallocated sectors and a failing disk spindle.

timeline.title: Backup Restoration Initiated
timeline.description: Team begins restoring the last verified backup to a clean replacement disk.

timeline.title: Service Restored
timeline.description: Data volume restored from backup. File system integrity verified and services restarted.

node-1.title: File System I/O Errors
node-1.situation: Application logs show repeated I/O errors on /dev/sdb1, the primary data volume. The database is rejecting writes and users cannot save data. The disk is mounted read-write and the server is still running.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Run SMART diagnostics on the disk, place the application in maintenance mode to prevent further writes, and notify stakeholders.

options.label: B
options.text: Run fsck on the mounted live filesystem to repair corruption without unmounting.

options.label: C
options.text: Ignore the errors and wait for the application to self-heal, as transient I/O errors are common.

node-2.title: SMART Diagnostics Results
node-2.situation: SMART diagnostics confirm the disk has 47 reallocated sectors and pending uncorrectable errors. The disk is physically failing. A replacement disk and last night's verified backup are available.
node-2.question: How do you proceed?

options.label: A
options.text: Unmount the corrupted volume, replace the disk, and begin restoring data from the last verified backup to the new disk.

options.label: B
options.text: Attempt to clone the failing disk to the replacement while it is still active, risking further corruption.

node-3.title: Backup Restoration
node-3.situation: The new disk is installed and the backup restoration has completed. Before bringing services back online, you need to verify data integrity.
node-3.question: What do you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Run fsck on the new volume, verify checksum integrity of restored files, and perform a database consistency check before restarting services.

options.label: B
options.text: Restart services immediately after restoration without verifying integrity to minimize downtime.

node-4.title: Service Restart and Validation
node-4.situation: Integrity checks passed. Services have been restarted and initial tests show data is accessible. You need to confirm the incident is fully resolved and prevent recurrence.
node-4.question: What is your next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Monitor disk health metrics, update the incident ticket with root cause and timeline, and review disk replacement and backup testing procedures.

options.label: B
options.text: Close the incident ticket without documenting the root cause to save time.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: You correctly identified the failing disk using SMART diagnostics, safely restored data from backup, verified file system integrity, and documented the incident with preventive recommendations.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
